title: Climate Justice & Equity
description: Who bears the burden of a warming planet? Explore the intersection of climate change, inequality, and human rights — from sinking islands to climate refugees.

stages.title: The Injustice of Climate Change
stages.body:
The countries most devastated by climate change contributed the least to causing it.

**The math is devastating:**
- The **richest 10%** of humanity produces **50%** of all emissions
- The **poorest 50%** produces just **12%**
- Small Island Developing States emit **<1%** of global CO₂ — yet face total extinction from sea-level rise
- Sub-Saharan Africa contributes **~3%** of emissions but bears **75%** of climate-related deaths

Climate change isn't just an environmental crisis. It's a **justice crisis**. It amplifies every existing inequality — poverty, gender, race, geography — and threatens to undo decades of human progress.

*"The people who have contributed the least to climate change are the ones suffering the most."* — Mary Robinson

stages.title: Voices from the Frontlines
stages.description: Meet the communities already living through the climate crisis. Click each card to learn their story.

cards.title: Tuvalu — Sinking Nation
cards.subtitle: Pacific Ocean · 12,000 people
cards.detail: Tuvalu's highest point is just 4.6 meters above sea level. Rising seas threaten to make the nation uninhabitable within decades. In 2023, Tuvalu signed a landmark deal with Australia offering climate migration pathways.

cards.title: Sahel Region — Desertification
cards.subtitle: Sub-Saharan Africa · 100M+ people
cards.detail: The Sahel is warming 1.5x faster than the global average. Lake Chad has shrunk by 90% since the 1960s, displacing millions and fueling conflict.

cards.title: South Asia — Extreme Heat
cards.subtitle: India & Pakistan · 1.8 billion people
cards.detail: 2025 saw wet-bulb temperatures exceeding human survivability limits in parts of South Asia. Outdoor laborers, farmers, and the urban poor are most exposed.

cards.title: Bangladesh — Flooding
cards.subtitle: Ganges Delta · 170 million people
cards.detail: One-third of Bangladesh sits less than 5 meters above sea level. By 2050, an estimated 13 million people could be displaced by rising waters and saltwater intrusion.

cards.title: Amazon Indigenous Communities
cards.subtitle: Brazil, Peru, Colombia · 1M+ indigenous people
cards.detail: Indigenous peoples protect 80% of world biodiversity. Deforestation, fires, and mining threaten their lands, knowledge systems, and ways of life that span millennia.

cards.title: Arctic Indigenous Peoples
cards.subtitle: Alaska, Canada, Siberia · 400,000 people
cards.detail: Arctic warming is 3-4x the global average. Permafrost thaw destroys infrastructure, sea ice loss threatens traditional hunting, and coastal erosion forces community relocations.

stages.title: What Kind of Justice?
stages.description: Climate justice means different things to different people. Which dimension matters most to you?

paths.label: Intergenerational Justice
paths.description: We're borrowing Earth from future generations. What debt are we leaving?

paths.label: Climate Reparations
paths.description: Should wealthy, high-emitting nations pay for damages in vulnerable countries?

paths.label: Energy Justice
paths.description: 1.2 billion people lack electricity. Can we decarbonize while lifting people out of poverty?

paths.label: Environmental Racism
paths.description: Pollution and climate impacts disproportionately affect communities of color worldwide.

stages.title: The Pledge vs. Reality Gap
stages.body:
In 2015, nearly every nation signed the **Paris Agreement**, pledging to limit warming to 1.5-2°C. But pledges are not policies, and policies are not action.

**Current reality:**
- National pledges put us on track for **~2.5-2.8°C** of warming — far above safe limits
- The **emissions gap** between pledges and 1.5°C targets: **~22 GtCO₂/year**
- Adaptation finance for developing countries: **~$21B delivered** vs. **$387B needed**

**The Loss and Damage Fund**, established at COP27 (2022) and operationalized at COP28 (2023), was a breakthrough — but contributions total only **~$700M** against estimated needs of **$400B/year by 2030**.

Justice isn't charity. It's about **acknowledging who caused the problem** and **who has the resources to fix it**.

questions.question: Which group produces 50% of global emissions?
questions.explanation: The richest 10% of humanity produces approximately 50% of all consumption-based CO₂ emissions (Oxfam/IPCC).

questions.question: What is a 'Loss and Damage' fund?
options: A fund for future space colonization
Compensation for climate damages in vulnerable nations
A savings account for future generations
questions.explanation: Loss and Damage refers to the financial mechanism compensating developing nations for climate impacts they did not cause.

questions.question: How many climate refugees are projected by 2050?
questions.explanation: The World Bank estimates 216 million people could be internally displaced by climate impacts by 2050.

questions.question: Arctic regions are warming how fast compared to the global average?
questions.explanation: Arctic amplification causes warming 3-4 times faster than the global average.

questions.question: What is the pledge vs. reality gap?
questions.hint: It's measured in degrees Celsius. Current pledges ≈ 2.7°C. Target = 1.5°C.

stages.title: Your Journey is Complete
stages.body:
You now understand climate change at every level:

🔬 **The Science** — Carbon atoms, the Keeling Curve, Earth's budget
💰 **The Economics** — Carbon markets, drawdown solutions, the cost of inaction
🌍 **The Justice** — Who suffers, who's responsible, and what fairness demands

This is the foundation for **COP31 in Antalya** and everything that follows. Knowledge without action is incomplete — but action without knowledge is dangerous.

**Earth doesn't need saving. It needs us to stop breaking it.**
